steps.title: Use the tax qualification rule
steps.content: During the Bourbon Restoration, voting required paying at least 300 francs in direct taxes. The passage then explains that real estate tax was the main means of voter qualification, even though relatively few people paid that tax.

steps.title: Infer from revenue evidence
steps.content: Real estate tax also produced more than two-thirds of government receipts. If a small group paid the tax, and that tax both dominated revenue and qualified people to vote, those voters most likely had enough French real estate to generate substantial tax payments.

steps.title: Reject unsupported inferences
steps.content:
• Choice B invents effects of artisans' and merchants' voting habits.
  • Choice C adds a trend in doors and windows after 1830 that the passage does not discuss.
  • Choice D ignores that people with foreign investments could still qualify through French real estate taxes.
The answer is A.
